problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem A (continuous): given a (hypothetical) newly-developed Ebola medication, build a realistic and useful model that addresses the spread of the disease, the quantity of medicine needed, possible feasible delivery systems, locations of delivery, speed of manufacturing of the vaccine/drug, and any other critical factors — then deliver a non-technical announcement letter for the world medical association. Team #32150 framed this as four coupled sub-problems: (1) model Ebola spread, (2) compute total medicine demand, (3) design a delivery/allocation system over the affected cities, and (4) analyze control & eradication levers — all anchored on real WHO/CDC outbreak data for Guinea, Sierra Leone and Liberia (the 2014 West Africa outbreak).
models_used: Baseline compartmental epidemic model: classic SEIR ODE system (Eq 4.1) with cumulative-case tracker CUM(t)=integral of sigma*E, used first as a sanity-check fit against WHO data

Time-varying transmission rate: beta(t) modeled as constant beta0 before control time tau, then exponential decay beta0*e^(-k(t-tau)) after interventions begin (Eq 4.2 / 4.7) — captures the effect of public-health measures

Custom extended compartmental model SEIHCR(CT) (Eq 4.3): 6 compartments S-E-I-H-C-R adding H=hospitalized, C=contaminated/infectious corpses, plus a Contact-Tracing removal term. Three separate transmission channels beta_I (community), beta_H (hospital), beta_C (corpse/funeral). This is the paper's central original model, adapted from Legrand/Rivers/Browne-style Ebola models

Next-generation matrix method for R0: R0 = spectral radius rho(F*V^-1) (Eq 4.5, van den Driessche & Watmough), yielding a closed-form three-term R0 = beta_I/(alpha+(1-delta_I)gamma_I+delta_I nu_I) + beta_H/((1-delta_H)gamma_H+delta_H nu_H) + beta_C/psi (Eq 4.6) — one term per transmission route

Least-squares curve fitting to estimate the unknown country-specific parameters (beta_I, beta_H, beta_C, alpha, psi) against WHO cumulative-case data; remaining parameters borrowed from cited literature
Medicine-demand model: D = CUM_p + v*T (Eq 5.1), equally weighting current cumulative patient load CUM_p and the disease's growth rate v over reporting period T

Weighted Fuzzy c-Means (FCM) clustering (Bezdek FCM, Eq 5.2-5.5) to site 6 delivery 'headquarters': standard FCM objective augmented with per-city medicine demand D_k as a weight, so cluster centers are pulled toward high-need cities; membership/centroid alternating-iteration update until ||U^(k+1)-U^(k)||<epsilon
Elbow-method model selection for cluster count c: plot objective J_m vs c, pick inflection point -> c=6 headquarters

Manufacturing-speed -> outcome coupling: piecewise model of in-hospital fatality delta_H as a function of production rate u vs demand D (Eq 5.6): delta_H = rho_u*(D-u)/D + delta_H0 when u<D, else floor delta_H0=20%
Medicine-efficacy coupling: recovery rate gamma_H = rho_eta*ln(eta+1) (Eq 5.7), and a linkage assumption gamma_H = theta*k with theta=3.2 tying cure rate to the transmission-decay rate

Control/eradication criteria expressed as calculus conditions on CUM(t): 'controlled' when CUM''<=0 and CUM'!=0 (Eq 7.1); 'eradicated' when CUM'=0 (Eq 7.2) — turns vague policy goals into checkable inequalities
Sensitivity analysis including Gaussian-noise injection (50% and 100%) on delta_H, plus parameter sweeps on beta_I, incubation 1/sigma
key_techniques: Progressive model building: deliberately start with textbook SEIR, show it already fits WHO data, then justify each added compartment (H, C) and the CT term by a concrete Ebola feature (hospital care, infectious corpses/funerals, contact tracing). The improvement is narrated, not just asserted
Three-channel force of infection lambda = beta_I*I/N + beta_H*H/N + beta_C*C/N, letting them isolate and later tune each transmission pathway (community vs hospital vs corpse) independently
Closed-form R0 decomposition by route via next-generation matrix — gives an interpretable, citable expression and a principled handle for the intervention analysis

Hybrid parameter strategy: fit the few identifiable/unknown parameters by least squares, borrow the rest from literature with citations — reduces overfitting risk and is honest about what is estimated vs assumed

Dual validation of medicine demand: compute total dose two independent ways (directly from WHO statistics, and from the SEIHCR(CT) model output for Freetown) and show they agree within acceptable error — a cross-check that builds judge trust
Making clustering 'part of the model' by feeding model-derived per-city demand D_k as FCM weights, so the logistics layer is coupled to the epidemic layer rather than bolted on
Reframing soft questions as derivative conditions: control = concavity of CUM (CUM''<=0), eradication = stationarity (CUM'=0); converts 'when is it controlled/eradicated' into quantitative thresholds

Region/threshold tables for policy: sweeping alpha, kappa, 1/gamma_H, delta_H and partitioning each into red (out of control) / blue (critical) / green (controlled) bands with explicit critical values (e.g. alpha critical ~0.32, kappa ~20) — directly actionable for decision-makers

Scenario quantification with a memorable headline number: cutting intervention introduction time tau from 210 to 145 days brings eradication ~200 days earlier; if production u<106.2 dose/day, fatality jumps from 20% to 57%
Tiered sensitivity reporting: classify each parameter as sensitive (beta_I) vs robust (incubation 1/sigma, noisy delta_H) instead of just dumping plots
Computational reproducibility: every figure caption lists the full parameter vector (N, beta_I0, beta_H, beta_C, alpha, gamma_I, gamma_H, nu_I, nu_H, psi, kappa, k, tau) used to generate it
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat this paper wins on completeness, validation, and decision-relevance. (1) It answers every clause of the prompt — spread, total quantity, delivery system, delivery locations, manufacturing speed, AND 'other critical factors' (corpses, contact tracing, isolation) — leaving no part of the question unaddressed, which is the single most common differentiator at the O level. (2) It earns credibility through repeated empirical validation: the SEIR and then SEIHCR(CT) curves are overlaid on real WHO cumulative-case data for all three countries and visibly track them (Sierra Leone nearly exactly), and medicine demand is independently corroborated two ways. Judges reward models that are shown to fit reality, not just derived. (3) It demonstrates genuine modeling maturity: a next-generation-matrix R0 with a clean per-route closed form signals the team understands the math, not just the mechanics of solving ODEs. (4) It is decision-useful: 6 concrete delivery headquarters with lat/long, percentage allocations per HQ and per country, a required production floor (106.2 dose/day), and red/blue/green control thresholds give a policymaker something to act on. (5) The narrative arc is disciplined — baseline -> justified improvement -> intervention analysis -> control/eradication -> sensitivity -> honest weaknesses -> a clean non-technical letter that restates results in plain language. That combination of real-data fit, theoretical depth, end-to-end coverage, and actionable output is exactly the O-award profile."
section_highlights: "Structure mirrors the problem and reads top-down. Strong elements: (a) A summary sheet that states each of the three/four results with concrete numbers (6 delivery locations, 106.2 dose/day, alpha 33-40% controls the disease, tau 210->145 saves 200 days) rather than vague claims. (b) A full Contents + Notations/Symbol Description table (every symbol defined once, with units like '1/sigma = average incubation') before any equations — so the dense ODE system is readable. (c) Explicit 'General Assumptions' section that scopes the model honestly (newborns excluded, recovered immune, medicine only in hospitals, straight-line cross-border delivery). (d) A 'Previous Research' literature review (Fisman, Gomes, CDC SIR, Chowell SEIR, Althaus, Valdez) that positions their model as a deliberate extension of prior Ebola work — signals scholarship. (e) Every figure caption is self-contained with its full parameter set, so results are reproducible. (f) Figure 14's red/green/blue control-region visualization plus Table 5 of critical values turns analysis into a decision aid. (g) The required Letter to the World Medical Association is genuinely non-technical, leads with empathy, and translates each technical result (6 HQs, allocation %, production speed, contact tracing) into lay recommendations. (h) Strengths/Weaknesses/Future Work is candid (admits parameter-coupling weakness and the single-city Freetown simplification) and points to a specific reference (Eisenberg) for the fix."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted weak coupling among the many parameters: beta_I, beta_H, beta_C are treated as independently tunable when physically they co-vary; the only linkages imposed (gamma_H=theta*k with theta=3.2 'based on experience', gamma_H=rho_eta*ln(eta+1)) are ad hoc and unjustified empirically
Several coupling coefficients are simply asserted (theta=3.2, rho_u=0.7, rho_eta=0.206, delta_H0=20%) with no derivation or source — convenient knobs that weaken the quantitative claims built on them

Identifiability/overfitting risk: 5 parameters per country fit by least squares against a single smooth cumulative curve; a good visual fit does not prove the SEIHCR(CT) structure is correct vs a simpler model (they never report fit error, confidence intervals, or compare AIC/residuals to SEIR)

Medicine-demand formula D = CUM_p + v*T equally weights stock (cumulative patients) and flow (growth rate) with admittedly arbitrary equal weights, and conflates 'cumulative patients' with current treatment demand (recovered/dead patients no longer need dosing) — likely over-counts

Delivery model is geometrically naive: assumes straight-line routes, every location can be a center, free cross-border transport, and ignores road networks, capacity, cold-chain, and travel time — FCM minimizes Euclidean distance, not real logistics cost

Eradication/control defined purely via CUM derivatives on a deterministic ODE; with stochastic late-stage dynamics a deterministic CUM'=0 is an idealization (real extinction is a stochastic event, as their own ref Valdez models)
Scope reduction: pharmaceutical demand from the model is computed for only ONE city (Freetown), then generalized — they flag this themselves as a source of discrepancy
kappa (contact-tracing intensity) is set to 0 in all the validation fits (Figs 4-6), so the headline 'CT' feature of the model is unvalidated against data and only exercised in forward what-if sweeps

Some figures/tables show data-entry or OCR-level inconsistencies (e.g. a longitude listed as 10.5202 E among W coordinates; control-region delta_H band '0.6-0.01') indicating light proofreading of the numeric results

Vaccine vs drug is never disambiguated — the medication is modeled only through fatality/recovery (treatment) effects, with no susceptible->removed vaccination flow, despite the prompt mentioning a vaccine
reusable_patterns: Baseline-then-extend modeling spine: fit a canonical model (SEIR) first, prove it works on real data, then add complexity only where a real feature of the problem demands it, narrating each addition. Reusable for any epidemiology/dynamics MCM problem

Compartmental-model toolkit for disease/diffusion problems: write the ODE system, derive R0 by the next-generation matrix rho(F*V^-1), and decompose R0 by transmission route for interpretability — a transferable recipe whenever a threshold/tipping quantity is needed
Hybrid parameterization: estimate only the non-identifiable parameters by least squares, cite the rest — a defensible, judge-friendly default for data-scarce continuous problems
Two-independent-routes validation: compute the key output quantity by two different methods (raw data vs model) and show agreement; cheap, powerful trust-builder

Coupling a logistics/optimization layer to the core model by feeding model outputs as weights (here per-city demand D_k into weighted FCM), so the whole solution is integrated rather than modular bolt-ons
Elbow method for choosing cluster count (plot objective vs k, take the knee) — directly reusable for any facility-location/clustering sub-question

Translate qualitative policy goals into calculus conditions on a cumulative curve: 'under control' = second derivative <= 0, 'eradicated' = first derivative = 0. A clean trick for any 'when does X stabilize/stop' question

Red/yellow/green decision-region tables: sweep each control lever, partition its range into out-of-control / critical / controlled bands with explicit critical thresholds — converts sensitivity sweeps into an actionable management dashboard
Self-contained figure captions carrying the full parameter vector used to generate each plot — makes results reproducible and signals rigor to judges
Tiered sensitivity classification (label parameters sensitive vs robust; inject Gaussian noise to test robustness) instead of undifferentiated parameter plots

The required-deliverable letter as plain-language mirror: open with empathy/audience framing, then restate each numeric result as a recommendation with no jargon — a template for the 'memo/letter/article' clause MCM problems often include
Honest Weaknesses + Future Work that names the specific limitation (parameter coupling, single-city simplification) and cites a paper that addresses it — disarms the judge's objections preemptively
